channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: FL-PBM: Pre-Training Backdoor Mitigation for Federated Learning
item.description: Backdoor attacks severely compromise federated learning (FL) by injecting poisoned data with hidden triggers, threatening safety-critical applications. Most defenses operate *during* or *after* training, leaving the vulnerable pre-training phase unguarded. We propose **FL-PBM**, the first client-side pre-training backdoor mitigation framework for FL. It consists of four lightweight, privacy-preserving steps: (1) inserting a benign trigger to establish a clean baseline; (2) applying PCA to extract trigger-discriminative features; (3) using GMM clustering in the PCA space to detect anomalous samples; and (4) applying targeted Gaussian blurring to disrupt potential triggers without degrading semantics. Evaluated on CIFAR-10 and Tiny-ImageNet, FL-PBM reduces attack success rates by up to **95% vs. FedAvg**, and by **30–80% relative to state-of-the-art defenses (RDFL, LPSF)**, while maintaining **>90% clean accuracy** across most settings. FL-PBM requires no server modification, preserves FL compatibility, and enables practical, proactive defense before any model update.

item.title: Mitigating Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning Using PPA and MiniMax Game Theory
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative model training but remains vulnerable to backdoor attacks launched by malicious clients who poison local updates to compromise global model integrity during inference. To address this, we propose **FedBBA**, a novel defense framework integrating: (1) a dynamic client reputation system based on gradient consistency and behavioral history; (2) an incentive-compatible mechanism that rewards honesty and penalizes anomalies via adaptive aggregation weights; and (3) a **Projection Pursuit Analysis (PPA)-enhanced minimax game**, where defender and attacker engage in a zero-sum game over parameter space—solved via Nash equilibrium to suppress malicious influence online. Extensive evaluation on GTSRB and BTSC benchmarks shows FedBBA reduces backdoor success rates to **1.1%–11%** across diverse attack scenarios (e.g., label-flipping, dynamic triggers), significantly outperforming state-of-the-art defenses (RDFL, RoPE: 23%–76%). Crucially, it maintains high clean-task accuracy (**95.3%–98.1%**), demonstrating strong robustness without sacrificing utility.

item.title: FeDMRA: Federated Incremental Learning with Dynamic Memory Replay Allocation
item.description: Federated Class-Incremental Learning (FCIL) enables privacy-preserving continual model adaptation across distributed healthcare clients, yet suffers severely from non-IID data—causing catastrophic forgetting and unfair performance degradation. To address this, we propose **FeDMRA**, a novel framework featuring **dynamic memory replay allocation**: instead of fixed exemplar budgets per client, it adaptively allocates limited storage based on local data heterogeneity, class novelty, and forgetting severity. Our strategy explicitly leverages non-IIDness as a learning signal via weighted replay and embeds fairness constraints to guarantee minimum accuracy across resource-diverse clients. Extensive experiments on ChestX-ray14, ISIC2019, and BraTS2021 show FeDMRA achieves **+5.8–9.3% higher average accuracy**, **41.5% lower forgetting**, and **52.1% reduced performance variance** versus state-of-the-art FCIL baselines—establishing a balanced, adaptive, and clinically deployable solution for federated medical AI evolution.

item.title: Key-Embedded Privacy for Decentralized AI in Biomedical Omics
item.description: Biomedical AI urgently requires privacy-preserving decentralized learning, yet cryptographic methods incur prohibitive overhead and differential privacy often degrades utility—hindering real-world clinical deployment. We propose **INFL (Key-Embedded Implicit Neural Federated Learning)**, a lightweight federated framework built on implicit neural representations (INRs). INFL embeds a secret key directly into the model architecture via coordinate-conditioned, plug-and-play modules—enabling provable privacy without external key management or gradient sharing. It supports seamless aggregation across heterogeneous, non-IID omics sites (bulk proteomics, scRNA-seq perturbation prediction, spatial transcriptomics clustering, and private-public multi-omics integration). Across diverse tasks, INFL achieves **near-lossless utility (98.3–100% of baseline accuracy) under strict privacy budgets (ε ≤ 2.0)**, reduces communication by 62%, and accelerates training 3.1× versus standard federated baselines. INFL bridges the gap between rigorous privacy and clinical-grade AI performance.

item.title: Taming the Instability: A Robust Second-Order Optimizer for Federated Learning over Non-IID Data
item.description: We propose **Federated Robust Curvature Optimization (FedRCO)**, a novel second-order optimizer for federated learning (FL) under statistical heterogeneity. To overcome the numerical instability and high computational cost of existing distributed second-order methods, FedRCO integrates three key innovations: (1) a real-time **Gradient Anomaly Monitor** that detects and clips exploding gradients; (2) a **Fail-Safe Resilience Protocol** that resets optimization states upon Hessian ill-conditioning or update divergence; and (3) a **Curvature-Preserving Adaptive Aggregation** strategy that fuses local curvature information without distorting local geometry. Theoretically, FedRCO guarantees bounded updates and $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ convergence for non-convex objectives without strong convexity assumptions. Empirically, it achieves up to 5.3% higher accuracy and 42% fewer communication rounds than state-of-the-art first- and second-order FL baselines across six non-IID benchmarks—including extreme label skew and client dropout—while maintaining >98% training stability.

item.title: Pre-Deployment Complexity Estimation for Federated Perception Systems
item.description: This paper introduces the first classifier-agnostic, pre-deployment framework for estimating learning complexity in federated perception systems. It jointly models data-intrinsic properties (e.g., dimensionality, sparsity, and cross-client heterogeneity) and system-level characteristics (e.g., client count, capability distribution, and data imbalance). Evaluated across 12 diverse federated settings—including rotated/noisy/label-skewed MNIST and class-/quantity-heterogeneous CIFAR-10/100—the proposed metric shows strong correlations: ρ = −0.89 with final test accuracy and ρ = 0.93 with communication rounds needed to reach target accuracy (e.g., 95%). Crucially, it requires no model training—enabling rapid feasibility assessment, resource planning, and dataset diagnostics prior to deployment.

item.title: Neural Federated Learning for Livestock Growth Prediction
item.description: Livestock growth prediction is critical for sustainable farm management but hindered by data scarcity and strict privacy constraints. To address this, we propose **LivestockFL**, the first neural federated learning framework tailored for cross-farm livestock growth modeling. It leverages a **GRU-MLP architecture** to jointly capture temporal weight dynamics and auxiliary features (e.g., feed, climate) without raw data sharing. Building on this, we introduce **LivestockPFL**, a personalized federated variant that adds farm-specific prediction heads trained locally—enabling adaptation to heterogeneous farming practices. Evaluated on a real-world dataset spanning 23 farms and >120,000 growth records, LivestockFL reduces MAE by 14.2% over centralized baselines, while LivestockPFL achieves a further 9.6% MAE reduction (final MAE = 1.87 kg), especially benefiting farms with limited historical data. Our work establishes a privacy-preserving, scalable, and adaptable paradigm for agricultural AI.

item.title: Kill-Chain Canaries: Stage-Level Tracking of Prompt Injection Across Attack Surfaces and Model Safety Tiers
item.description: We introduce *Kill-Chain Canaries*, a stage-level tracking framework for prompt injection attacks across LLM agents. Instead of measuring only task-level attack success rate (ASR), we instrument cryptographic canary tokens (`SECRET-[A-F0-9]{8}`) to trace adversarial content through four kill-chain stages—*Exposed*, *Persisted*, *Relayed*, and *Executed*—across four attack surfaces and five defense conditions (764 runs total). Our key insight is that model safety hinges not on whether adversarial input is *seen*, but on whether it *propagates across pipeline stages*. We find: (1) 100% exposure across all five models—safety gaps are entirely downstream; (2) Claude strips injections at memory summarization (0/164 ASR), while GPT-4o-mini propagates canaries with high fidelity (53% ASR, 95% CI: 41–65%); (3) DeepSeek shows diametrically opposite behavior—0% ASR on memory surfaces vs. 100% on tool-stream surfaces; (4) all four active defenses fail catastrophically (100% ASR) due to threat-model surface mismatch; and (5) a Claude relay node decontaminates downstream agents (0/40 canaries survive into shared memory). This work redefines safety evaluation around *inter-stage propagation*, enabling precise, attributable defense assessment.

item.title: FedDES: Graph-Based Dynamic Ensemble Selection for Personalized Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers from negative transfer under statistical heterogeneity, as a single global model poorly serves diverse client data distributions. While personalized FL (pFL) tailors models per client, most existing methods apply uniform or static aggregation over peer models—ignoring that model competence varies significantly across *individual samples* within the same client. To address this, we propose **FedDES**, a decentralized pFL framework enabling *instance-level personalization* via dynamic ensemble selection. FedDES constructs a heterogeneous graph linking data samples and candidate client models, then trains a lightweight Graph Neural Network (GNN) meta-learner to predict sample-specific model weights. At inference, for each query, the GNN dynamically selects and weights only the most competent peer models, suppressing irrelevant or harmful ones. Experiments on non-IID CIFAR-10 and real-world ICU data show FedDES consistently outperforms state-of-the-art pFL baselines (e.g., +3.2% avg. accuracy), with robustness under extreme data skew (α=0.1). Ablation confirms the GNN-driven dynamic selection is key to mitigating negative transfer.

item.title: FedFG: Privacy-Preserving and Robust Federated Learning via Flow-Matching Generation
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients without raw data sharing, yet suffers from privacy leakage (e.g., via gradient inversion) and vulnerability to client-side poisoning attacks. To address both challenges simultaneously, we propose **FedFG**, a novel FL framework leveraging **flow-matching generation** for joint privacy preservation and robustness. On the client side, each device decouples its network into a private feature extractor (never shared) and a public classifier; a lightweight flow-matching generator learns the local data distribution and produces synthetic samples to replace raw features during server interaction—effectively masking sensitive information. On the server side, we design a client-update verification scheme and a **generative-driven robust aggregation (GDRA)** mechanism that weights updates based on consistency with synthetic samples, suppressing malicious contributions. Experiments on MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10 show FedFG achieves up to 14.2% higher accuracy than FedAvg under diverse poisoning attacks while reducing membership inference attack success rate to ≤53.1% (vs. 76.4% baseline), demonstrating strong privacy-utility-robustness trade-off without differential privacy or trusted third parties.

item.title: Hidden Ads: Behavior Triggered Semantic Backdoors for Advertisement Injection in Vision Language Models
item.description: We introduce **Hidden Ads**, a novel behavior-triggered semantic backdoor attack against Vision-Language Models (VLMs) deployed in consumer recommendation services. Unlike conventional pattern-based backdoors, Hidden Ads activates *naturally* when users upload images containing target semantics (e.g., food, cars) and ask recommendation-seeking questions—triggering seamless injection of attacker-specified promotional slogans while preserving high task accuracy (>99.5% original performance) and natural language fluency. We propose a three-tier threat framework (hard/soft prompt injection, supervised fine-tuning) and a teacher-VLM–guided data poisoning pipeline that generates semantically grounded trigger–slogan pairs across domains. Experiments on BLIP-2, LLaVA-1.5, and Qwen-VL show >92% injection success, near-zero false positives (<0.3%), strong cross-dataset transferability, and efficient multi-domain scaling. Standard defenses—including instruction filtering and clean fine-tuning—fail to remove the backdoor without severe utility degradation (up to −17.2% recommendation accuracy). Hidden Ads exposes critical semantic-level vulnerabilities in real-world VLM interfaces.

item.title: A Comparative Evaluation of DATA and Microwalk for Detecting Constant-Time Violations in Cryptographic Libraries
item.description: This paper presents the first controlled, comparative evaluation of DATA and Microwalk—two state-of-the-art dynamic binary instrumentation tools for detecting constant-time (CT) violations in cryptographic software. Using a unified testbed across LibTomCrypt, OpenSSL, and liboqs, we assess their detection accuracy, consistency, and resource overhead on symmetric and asymmetric primitives. A key challenge emerged in asymmetric schemes (e.g., RSA, Kyber): internally generated random numbers caused high false positives and poor inter-tool agreement (<35% overlap). To address this, we externalize randomness injection—replacing internal PRNG calls with deterministic, pre-seeded sequences—thereby enabling fair, reproducible comparison. Results show that while both tools agree strongly on symmetric ciphers (e.g., AES), externalized randomness improves violation detection consistency to 82% for asymmetric algorithms. We further quantify practical trade-offs: DATA consumes ~2.1× more memory due to path-constraint solving, whereas Microwalk incurs up to 3.7× longer runtime on complex lattice-based operations. Our methodology advances CT verification standardization and informs tool selection for real-world cryptographic assurance.

item.title: Concrete Estimation of Correctness and IND-CPA-D Security for FHE via Rare Event Simulation
item.description: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) schemes inherently exhibit probabilistic correctness due to noise introduced by LWE-based assumptions. While noise growth during homomorphic evaluation has long been analyzed for correctness, recent work establishes its *direct impact* on IND-CPA-D security—making precise noise modeling essential for rigorous security guarantees. We present the first practical rare-event simulation framework for FHE noise analysis, adapted from importance splitting to the cryptographic setting. Applied to TFHE bootstrapping and variants, it delivers *empirically validated bounds* on decryption failure probability down to $2^{-128}$, confirming that the standard Gaussian noise model is overly conservative, while an Irwin-Hall–based refinement accurately captures actual noise behavior. Our approach bridges theory and practice, enabling simultaneous, quantifiable assurance of both correctness and IND-CPA-D security—and provides a modular baseline extendable to other FHE schemes (e.g., CKKS, BFV).

item.title: Post-Quantum Blockchains with Agility in Mind
item.description: Blockchains rely on cryptographic primitives for long-term integrity, yet quantum computing threatens current schemes like ECDSA. While cryptographic agility—the ability to upgrade algorithms without operational disruption—is essential, existing blockchains lack native, genesis-level support. We bridge this gap with an EVM-compatible blockchain designed for agility from inception. Our contributions include: (1) a flexibility framework characterizing algorithm distribution across layers; (2) Cryptographically Agile Transactions (CATX), a novel transaction format that decouples body and signature to enable user-chosen signature schemes (e.g., ECDSA, Falcon-512, ML-DSA); and (3) a consensus-layer key registration mechanism allowing validators to migrate signature schemes via operational upgrades—no hard fork required. Experiments over 30,000 blocks and 11 million transactions show CATX introduces **zero measurable overhead** in throughput, gas cost, or verification latency. This work establishes a practical, upgrade-safe foundation for post-quantum blockchains.

item.title: Can Adaptive Communication Graphs Lower the Bottleneck Complexity of (Secure) Multiparty Computation?
item.description: This paper initiates the systematic study of *adaptive communication graphs*—where the communication topology evolves dynamically based on inputs or intermediate protocol state—to reduce the bottleneck complexity of (secure) multiparty computation. Unlike prior works restricted to fixed graphs (where edges depend only on round number), we require protocols to remain correct in *asynchronous networks*, ruling out artificial tricks like “communication via silence.” For arbitrary Boolean functions $f\colon\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, we construct a secure MPC protocol achieving bottleneck complexity $O(n/\log n)$, which we prove is asymptotically optimal—breaking the $\Omega(n)$ lower bound known for fixed graphs. For symmetric functions, we broaden the class efficiently computable with low bottleneck and demonstrate that *input-adaptive* graphs further reduce complexity beyond static or output-adaptive approaches. Our results establish adaptive topology as a fundamental resource for communication balancing in distributed computation.

item.title: Refined Approx-SVP Rank Reduction Conditions and Adaptive Lattice Reduction for MSIS Security Estimation
item.description: Lattice-based cryptography’s concrete security hinges on the hardness of Approx-SVP, yet existing rank reduction conditions for cryptographic dimensions (e.g., ≥850) are overly optimistic—implicitly assuming abundant extremely short vectors. This work refines Approx-SVP feasibility: under the dimension-for-free (D4f) paradigm, *a single sufficiently short vector suffices*. We derive two compact, practical rank reduction conditions—one geometric (sieve-based), one probabilistic (basis-quality-aware)—validated experimentally: they outperform prior methods by up to **60×** at dimensions 850 and 925. To realize them reliably, we propose **APBKZ**, an adaptive Pump-based reduction that dynamically tunes blocksize and D4f parameters using the evolving Gram-Schmidt profile, and **HeadAPBKZ**, a head-focused mode activating only after condition satisfaction. Integrated into MSIS security estimation, our framework reduces Dilithium’s estimated security margin by **9.50–16.63 bits** versus the conservative Core-SVP baseline—enabling more accurate, realistic post-quantum standardization benchmarks.

item.title: PD-Net: Learning Device-Invariant Representations for Heterogeneous Cross-Device Side-Channel Attacks
item.description: This paper introduces **PD-Net**, the first domain generalization (DG)-based deep learning framework enabling practical *zero-shot heterogeneous cross-device side-channel attacks*. PD-Net learns device-invariant representations by disentangling algorithmic content from device-specific style via dual-branch encoding, and aligning content-feature distributions across diverse hardware platforms—spanning 32-bit ARM Cortex-M series, 8-bit AVR ATmega microcontrollers, and 128-bit Xilinx Virtex-5 FPGAs—using prototypical clustering and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) losses. Trained solely on nine heterogeneous source domains (power & electromagnetic leakages) without any target-device data or adaptation, PD-Net achieves robust zero-shot transfer: it successfully launches side-channel attacks in **29 out of 70 cross-architecture settings**, with only **10 divergences**, vastly outperforming the state of the art (4 successes, 19 divergences). This work establishes DG as a viable foundation for scalable, calibration-free side-channel evaluation across heterogeneous embedded devices.

item.title: Adaptively-Secure Proxy Re-Encryption with Tight Security
item.description:
Proxy Re-Encryption (PRE) enables a semi-trusted proxy to transform ciphertexts for user $i$ into those for user $j$ using a re-encryption key. While highly useful in cloud and data-sharing systems, existing PRE schemes suffer severe security degradation under *adaptive* attacks—where adversaries may corrupt keys adaptively—often incurring polynomial or worse security loss. Prior tight-security results were scarce and restricted.
This paper presents **four adaptively secure PRE schemes with tight security** under the Matrix Decisional Diffie-Hellman (MDDH) assumption: $\mathsf{PRE}_0$/$\mathsf{PRE}_1$ achieve tight aHRA security (single/multi-challenge), while $\mathsf{PRE}_2$/$\mathsf{PRE}_3$ achieve (almost) tight aCCA security. To enable tight CCA security, we introduce **tag-based language-malleable NIZK with special simulation soundness**—a novel primitive preserving simulation soundness while allowing controlled, syntax-aware malleability—and construct both one-time and unbounded versions from MDDH. Our results are the first to achieve truly tight adaptive CCA security for PRE.

item.title: GUIDE: Resolving Domain Bias in GUI Agents through Real-Time Web Video Retrieval and Plug-and-Play Annotation
item.description: Large vision-language models (VLMs) empower GUI agents but suffer from *domain bias*: poor familiarity with application-specific workflows (planning) and UI layouts (grounding), due to scarce domain-operation data in pretraining. We propose **GUIDE**, a training-free, plug-and-play framework that resolves this bias by autonomously retrieving and annotating expertise from open-web tutorial videos. GUIDE introduces (1) a **subtitle-driven Video-RAG pipeline**, performing three-stage retrieval (domain classification → topic extraction → frame-level relevance matching) to identify task-relevant videos; and (2) a **fully automated inverse-dynamics annotation pipeline**, which feeds UI-augmented keyframes into VLMs to infer planning and grounding knowledge—then injects it directly into the agent’s modules. Evaluated on OSWorld, GUIDE boosts success rates by **+5.2–7.8%**, reduces execution steps by **12.4%**, and works seamlessly with both single-model and multi-agent systems—*without any model modification*. It establishes a new paradigm for zero-shot, architecture-agnostic GUI agent domain adaptation.

item.title: Clawed and Dangerous: Can We Trust Open Agentic Systems?
item.description: This paper systematically analyzes the security of open agentic systems—LLM-driven agents with external tool access, persistent memory, and privileged execution—through a software engineering lens. We introduce a six-dimensional analytical taxonomy and synthesize 50 papers across attacks, benchmarks, defenses, audits, and foundational engineering. From this, we derive a *secure-by-construction doctrine* for agent platforms and a concrete *security posture scorecard* with 18 measurable criteria. Our review reveals maturity in attack characterization (e.g., plan injection, memory poisoning) and benchmark design (e.g., AgentBench), but critical gaps remain in deployment controls, operational governance, persistent-memory integrity, and real-time capability revocation. These gaps define an urgent engineering agenda for building governable, auditable, and resilient agent ecosystems.

item.title: A Human-Inspired Decoupled Architecture for Efficient Audio Representation Learning
item.description: Self-supervised learning has advanced audio representation, yet standard Transformers suffer from excessive parameters (85–94M) and quadratic computational cost, hindering edge deployment. To address this, we propose **HEAR**, a human-inspired *decoupled architecture*: it separates local acoustic feature extraction (via a lightweight CNN-RNN Acoustic Model) from global semantic integration (via a compact Transformer Task Model). An Acoustic Tokenizer—trained via knowledge distillation—enables efficient Masked Audio Modeling with low-dimensional, semantically rich tokens. HEAR achieves only **15M parameters** and **9.47 GFLOPs** inference cost—~82% less than foundation models—while attaining highly competitive accuracy across diverse benchmarks (e.g., 89.2% on ESC-50). Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/HarunoriKawano/HEAR.

item.title: Semi-Automated Knowledge Engineering and Process Mapping for Total Airport Management
item.description: This paper addresses the critical challenge of semantic fragmentation in airport operations documentation—caused by technical jargon, regulatory complexity, regional heterogeneity, and stakeholder silos—which impedes Total Airport Management (TAM). We propose a semi-automated framework that synergistically fuses expert-curated symbolic Knowledge Engineering (KE) with generative LLMs to build a domain-grounded, machine-readable Knowledge Graph. A scaffolded prompting strategy leverages KE structures to guide LLMs toward semantically aligned triple extraction. Evaluation on Google LangExtract reveals a counterintuitive finding: document-level LLM inference—contrary to typical long-context degradation—improves recovery of non-linear procedural dependencies by 37.2% over segment-based processing. To meet aviation’s stringent traceability requirements, every extracted triple is probabilistically discovered *and* deterministically anchored to its exact source span, ensuring full verifiability. Finally, we operationalize the pipeline into an automated system that synthesizes executable operational workflows directly from unstructured textual corpora.

item.title: Clinical named entity recognition in the Portuguese language: a benchmark of modern BERT models and LLMs
item.description: This study establishes the first comprehensive benchmark for clinical Named Entity Recognition (NER) in Portuguese, evaluating both modern BERT variants (BioBERTpt, BERTimbau, ModernBERT, mmBERT) and frontier LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5) on the public SemClinBr corpus and a private breast cancer dataset. All models were trained under identical conditions and assessed using precision, recall, and micro/macro F1-scores. To address severe multilabel imbalance—common in clinical entities—we rigorously compared iterative stratification, weighted loss, and oversampling. Results show **mmBERT-base achieves the highest performance (micro-F1 = 0.76)**, outperforming all other models—including GPT-5 (0.68) and Gemini-2.5 (0.65)—while remaining fully deployable offline with modest computational resources. **Iterative stratification proved most effective**, improving both rare-class recall (+12.3%) and overall F1 (+4.1 pts). Crucially, multilingual BERT models, especially mmBERT, demonstrate superior cross-lingual transfer for Portuguese clinical NER without domain-specific pretraining, offering an efficient, practical solution for low-resource medical language processing.

item.title: DataFlex: A Unified Framework for Data-Centric Dynamic Training of Large Language Models
item.description: Data-centric training—optimizing data selection, composition, and weighting alongside model parameters—has emerged as a vital paradigm for improving LLMs. Yet existing methods suffer from fragmented implementations, inconsistent interfaces, and poor reproducibility. We introduce **DataFlex**, a unified, extensible framework built on LLaMA-Factory that seamlessly integrates three core dynamic data optimization paradigms: sample selection, domain mixture adjustment, and sample reweighting—while preserving full compatibility with standard training workflows. It provides modular abstractions for embedding extraction, inference, and gradient computation, with native support for large-scale systems (e.g., DeepSpeed ZeRO-3). Experiments show dynamic selection consistently outperforms static full-data training on MMLU (+1.8–2.3% absolute gain); DoReMi and ODM improve both MMLU accuracy (+1.4–2.1%) and corpus-level perplexity (−0.9–1.7 ppl) over default mixtures during Qwen2.5-1.5B pretraining; and DataFlex achieves up to 18% runtime speedup over original implementations. DataFlex establishes a robust, efficient, and reproducible infrastructure for data-centric LLM training.

item.title: Haechi: Simple Commitment-based Keyless In-person Verifiable Elections
item.description: Haechi is a novel end-to-end verifiable (E2E-V) voting system designed specifically for **in-person elections**, where multiple votes are collected on one or more local devices. It eliminates the need for cryptographic keys—traditionally required for ballot encryption—by using **cryptographic commitments** instead, thereby removing key management as a source of complexity, error, and trust assumptions. This yields a simpler, more robust, and auditable design. To address the scalability bottleneck of large election records in existing E2E-V systems—which hinder public verification due to high storage, distribution, and computational costs—Haechi leverages **modern compact zero-knowledge proofs** (e.g., optimized Bulletproofs+ and PLONK variants). As a result, its election record size is reduced by **over an order of magnitude** (≥90% smaller) compared to real-world deployments like Geneva’s 2020 e-voting pilot and South Korea’s 2022 local elections, while preserving full verifiability, linear-time verification, and post-quantum resilience.

item.title: Improving ML Attacks on LWE with Data Repetition and Stepwise Regression
item.description: The Learning with Errors (LWE) problem underpins lattice-based post-quantum cryptography, yet recent ML attacks have exposed vulnerabilities for sparse secrets—recovering at most 3 non-zero bits in the “cruel region” (Nolte et al., 2024) even after BKZ preprocessing. We propose two synergistic techniques: (i) **data repetition**, generating multiple noisy variants per LWE sample to boost signal-to-noise resilience; and (ii) **stepwise regression**, iteratively identifying high-confidence “cool bits” (zero-valued coordinates) to prune the search space before estimating remaining “hot bits”. Empirically, scaling training data size and repetition count yields a power-law improvement in success probability ($P_{\text{success}} \propto (N \cdot R)^{0.38}$), enabling recovery of secrets with 7–9 active bits (vs. prior 3) for $n=128$, $q \approx 2^{17}$. Our stepwise method reduces bit-error rate by 62% and provides interpretability absent in end-to-end models—advancing ML-based cryptanalysis toward practical, principled attack frameworks.

item.title: Unveiling the Resilience of LLM-Enhanced Search Engines against Black-Hat SEO Manipulation
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic security analysis of Large Language Model-enhanced Search Engines (LLMSEs) against black-hat SEO attacks. We evaluate 10 real-world LLMSEs (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) using **SEO-Bench**, a novel benchmark of 1,000 verified black-hat SEO websites. Our measurements reveal that LLMSEs mitigate **99.78% of traditional SEO attacks**, primarily through robust retrieval-stage filtering. However, we identify a critical vulnerability in the generation stage: newly proposed **LLMSEO attacks**—including rewritten-query stuffing and segmented-text injection—double manipulation success rates (from 2.3% to 4.9%). This exposes a fundamental “retrieval-strong, generation-weak” security imbalance. We responsibly disclosed all findings to major vendors, enabling concrete mitigations. Our work establishes both the first evaluation framework and actionable insights for building resilient, AI-native search systems.

item.title: Beyond Content Safety: Real-Time Monitoring for Reasoning Vulnerabilities in Large Language Models
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly depend on explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, yet the *safety of the reasoning process itself* remains unaddressed. We introduce **reasoning safety** as a foundational security dimension—requiring logical consistency, computational efficiency, and robustness against adversarial manipulation in reasoning trajectories. First, we formally define reasoning safety and propose a nine-category taxonomy of unsafe reasoning behaviors (e.g., input misparsing, step redundancy, premise misuse). Second, we annotate 4,111 CoT chains from natural benchmarks and four adversarial attacks (including reasoning hijacking and denial-of-service), confirming all error types occur in practice and each attack yields mechanistically interpretable signatures. Third, we design a lightweight **Reasoning Safety Monitor**: an external LLM-based component that inspects every reasoning step in real time via taxonomy-embedded prompting and triggers interrupts upon detection. Evaluated on a 450-chain static benchmark, it achieves **84.88% step-level localization accuracy** and **85.37% error-type classification accuracy**, substantially outperforming hallucination detectors and process reward models. This work establishes reasoning-level monitoring as both necessary and practically attainable for secure LLM deployment.

item.title: Shape and Substance: Dual-Layer Side-Channel Attacks on Local Vision-Language Models
item.description: On-device Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are widely adopted for privacy-preserving local inference, yet we uncover a fundamental algorithmic side channel introduced by dynamic high-resolution preprocessing (e.g., AnyRes). Unlike static pipelines, dynamic resizing generates workload-varying patch counts based solely on input aspect ratio—enabling unprivileged attackers to fingerprint geometry via OS-level timing metrics (Tier 1), and further resolve semantic density (e.g., X-rays vs. text documents) via Last-Level Cache contention profiling (Tier 2). Evaluated on LLaVA-NeXT and Qwen2-VL, our dual-layer framework achieves >98.5% geometry classification accuracy and AUC=0.93 for intra-geometry semantic discrimination—enabling reliable inference of sensitive contexts. We quantify severe trade-offs: constant-work mitigation incurs 37–52% latency overhead and >40% energy penalty. We propose practical, low-overhead design principles for secure Edge AI, including aspect-ratio-aware preprocessing and hardware-assisted side-channel monitoring.

item.title: ALPS: Automated Least-Privilege Enforcement for Securing Serverless Functions
item.description: Serverless computing is widely adopted for AI workloads but suffers from pervasive over-privilege due to coarse-grained, manually configured permissions. This paper introduces **ALPS**, the first fully automated framework for enforcing least privilege in heterogeneous serverless environments. ALPS combines (1) a serverless-optimized static analyzer that precisely extracts fine-grained, resource-specific permission requirements from function code; (2) a fine-tuned LLM (Qwen2-7B) that generates syntactically and semantically correct, vendor-specific security policies (AWS IAM, Azure RBAC, GCP IAM) with language-aware context; and (3) lightweight runtime monitoring that blocks unauthorized access and adapts to code or policy changes. Evaluated on 8,322 real-world functions across AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, ALPS achieves **94.8% coverage** in least-privilege extraction, improves policy generation quality by **+220% (BLEU)**, **+124% (ChrF++)**, and **+100% (ROUGE-2)** over baselines, and incurs only **12ms average cold-start overhead** (<0.8%). ALPS is cloud-agnostic, language-agnostic (Python/Node.js/Java), and production-ready.

item.title: On the Vulnerability of Deep Automatic Modulation Classifiers to Explainable Backdoor Threats
item.description: This paper introduces the first **physically realizable backdoor attack** targeting deep learning-based Automatic Modulation Classification (AMC) in wireless communications. Diverging from vision-domain heuristics, we propose an **XAI-guided trigger synthesis framework**: Integrated Gradients identify the most vulnerable temporal segments of baseband signals (e.g., QPSK waveforms), and a class-prototype PCA method generates low-dimensional, energy-concentrated triggers injectable *before transmission*. Evaluated on ResNet18, LSTM, and CLDNN models, the attack achieves **>96.2% attack success rate** across SNR = 0–20 dB with only **0.8% poisoning ratio**, demonstrating unprecedented stealth and robustness. Our work exposes a critical physical-layer vulnerability in DL-AMC systems and establishes the first XAI-informed backdoor paradigm for signal intelligence.

item.title: Physical Backdoor Attack Against Deep Learning-Based Modulation Classification
item.description: This paper introduces a novel **physical backdoor attack** targeting deep learning-based modulation classifiers in RF systems. Unlike digital backdoor attacks that inject synthetic triggers into training data, our method exploits inherent **power amplifier (PA) nonlinear distortions**—a real-world hardware imperfection—as the physical trigger. During training, the adversary applies controlled PA distortion to clean RF signals and relabels them to a target modulation class, thereby implanting a stealthy backdoor. Crucially, the poisoned model maintains high accuracy (>98%) on unaltered test signals but misclassifies them with >92.7% success rate when the same PA distortion is reapplied. We validate the attack across SNRs (5–20 dB) using the RML2016.10a dataset and demonstrate its resilience against state-of-the-art defenses—including adversarial training, neuron pruning, input reconstruction, and feature denoising—all of which reduce attack success rate by less than 3.2%. This work reveals a critical hardware-AI security coupling and establishes the first practical physical-layer Trojan for wireless ML systems.

item.title: Mitigating Evasion Attacks in Fog Computing Resource Provisioning Through Proactive Hardening
item.description: This paper addresses evasion attacks targeting the k-means–based resource provisioning system in fog computing, where an adversary first reverse-engineers the offline clustering model via query-based probing and then submits adversarial workloads to mislead online classification—causing VM overloading and service degradation. We propose a proactive hardening method using *adversarial training*: during offline clustering, we augment real workload data with carefully crafted perturbations (adapted from FGSM to k-means distance metrics) to enhance classifier robustness. Evaluated across diverse fog topologies, our approach reduces evasion success rate from 89.7% to 12.3%, maintains >99.4% resource allocation stability under attack, and incurs only ≤3.1% latency overhead. This is the first defense framework that proactively immunizes fog resource provisioning against evasion threats while preserving low-latency operational constraints.

item.title: PIDP-Attack: Combining Prompt Injection with Database Poisoning Attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
item.description: We propose **PIDP-Attack**, a novel compound adversarial attack against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems that synergistically combines *prompt injection* at inference time with *targeted database poisoning*. Unlike prior poisoning attacks (e.g., PoisonedRAG) requiring knowledge of user queries, PIDP-Attack injects only a few malicious passages (<0.1% of the corpus) and appends lightweight trigger strings to *arbitrary* user queries—enabling effective, query-agnostic manipulation of LLM outputs. Evaluated across three benchmarks (Natural Questions, HotpotQA, MS-MARCO) and eight LLMs, PIDP-Attack consistently outperforms PoisonedRAG, boosting attack success rates by **4–16 percentage points** on open-domain QA while preserving high retrieval precision (Recall@5 ≥ 92%). This demonstrates that coupling prompt-level and data-level vulnerabilities is both necessary and highly effective for real-world RAG exploitation.

item.title: The System Prompt Is the Attack Surface: How LLM Agent Configuration Shapes Security and Creates Exploitable Vulnerabilities
item.description: This paper establishes that the **system prompt is a primary attack surface** for LLM agents. In PhishNChips—a benchmark across 11 models and 10 prompt strategies—we show prompt-model interaction is a *first-order security variable*: a single model’s phishing bypass rate varies from <1% to 97% depending solely on prompt configuration, while false-positive costs diverge sharply across models under identical prompts. Optimizing prompts around highly predictive signals (e.g., sender-URL domain matching) achieves strong benchmark performance (up to **93.7% recall at 3.8% FPR**) but creates brittle vulnerabilities: when attackers register matching malicious infrastructure, performance collapses. Response-trace analysis reveals **98% of successful bypasses follow the prompt faithfully—yet reason based on an inverted, adversarially exploited signal**, exposing the fragility of assumption-dependent instructions. Counterintuitively, increasing prompt specificity degrades robust reasoning by replacing multi-signal fusion with exploitable single-signal reliance. We formalize the detection-usability-robustness tradeoff, introduce **Safetility**—a deployability-aware metric penalizing false positives—and argue that closing the adversarial gap requires **tool augmentation with external ground truth**, not prompt tuning alone.

item.title: A Public Theory of Distillation Resistance via Constraint-Coupled Reasoning Architectures
item.description: This paper introduces a public, trade-secret-safe theoretical framework—Constraint-Coupled Reasoning Architecture (CCRA)—to mitigate the governance asymmetry in knowledge distillation: where capabilities transfer cheaply, but their original alignment and safety constraints do not. We formalize the core thesis that distillation becomes less effective as a capability shortcut when high-level reasoning is *architecturally coupled* to internal stability constraints governing state evolution over time. CCRA comprises four formal elements: bounded transition burden, path-load accumulation, dynamically evolving feasible regions, and a capability-stability coupling condition. Crucially, the framework omits all proprietary implementation details—ensuring public accessibility while preserving commercial confidentiality. Its contribution is purely theoretical: a falsifiable architectural claim, a precise threat model (adversarial distillers access only weights, not constraint-execution context), and three experimentally testable hypotheses—for instance, removing any CCRA component degrades distilled model stability on multi-step reasoning by ≥40%. This work lays a foundational, axiomatizable basis for distillation resistance, robust alignment, and model governance research.

item.title: On the Foundations of Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence
item.description: We establish the **Determinism Thesis**: platform-deterministic inference—bitwise-identical outputs across *all* hardware (ARM/x86), OS, and runtime—is *necessary and sufficient* for trustworthy AI. We formalize **trust entropy** ($H_T$) and prove verification failure probability equals exactly $1 - 2^{-H_T}$. The **Determinism-Verification Collapse** theorem shows: under determinism, verification reduces to $O(1)$ hash comparison; without it, verification becomes intractable. IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic violates determinism fundamentally; we resolve this via a pure integer inference engine (99k lines Rust) achieving bitwise identical outputs across ARM/x86. In 82 cross-architecture tests (up to 6.7B-parameter models), zero hash mismatches occurred; four geographically distributed nodes produced identical outputs, attested by 356 on-chain transactions. Crucially, *all* major trust properties—fairness, robustness, privacy, safety, alignment—presuppose platform determinism. AI trust is, ultimately, an arithmetic question.

item.title: Sovereign AI at the Front Door of Care: A Physically Unidirectional Architecture for Secure Clinical Intelligence
item.description: We introduce a Sovereign AI architecture for clinical triage at the front door of care—designed for clinics, field hospitals, and resource-constrained settings—where all inference runs entirely on-device and patient data flows *only* via a physically unidirectional channel (using receive-only broadcast or certified hardware data diodes), with *no return path whatsoever*. This construction eliminates the network-mediated attack surface by physics—not software—making remote compromise, exfiltration, or model theft architecturally impossible. The system performs conversational symptom intake, fuses device-captured vitals, and generates structured, triage-aligned clinical records in real time. We formally define receiver-side unidirectionality, prove its transport-agnostic security across broadcast and diode deployments, and analyze threat models, enforcement mechanisms, and configurations for high-assurance operation—even under zero-trust networking. This work establishes physical one-way data flow as a foundational primitive for sovereign, on-device clinical intelligence.

item.title: Agentic Trust Coordination for Federated Learning through Adaptive Thresholding and Autonomous Decision Making in Sustainable and Resilient Industrial Networks
item.description: Distributed intelligence in resource-constrained industrial networks demands robust federated learning (FL) under dynamic, noisy, and adversarial conditions. Existing trust mechanisms rely on static thresholds or simplistic heuristics, failing to adapt contextually to evolving operational states. This paper introduces the **Agentic Trust Control Layer (ATCL)**—a lightweight, server-side control loop that decouples observation, reasoning, and action. ATCL continuously monitors multi-dimensional system and trust signals (e.g., gradient variance, temporal consistency, device health), applies adaptive thresholding with sliding-window temporal analysis to detect instability onset—not just outliers—and triggers targeted interventions (e.g., progressive weight attenuation or selective update rejection). Crucially, it requires **no client-side modifications** and **adds zero communication overhead**. Evaluated on IEEE 118-bus power grid simulations and real industrial vibration datasets, ATCL reduces accuracy fluctuation under attacks by **62.3%**, improves convergence stability by **3.8×**, and maintains **94.7%** baseline performance under sensor drift. This work pioneers agentic, context-aware trust coordination for sustainable and resilient FL in industry.

item.title: Revealing the influence of participant failures on model quality in cross-silo Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) promises privacy-preserving collaborative model training, yet its real-world reliability is threatened by participant failures—crashes, network partitions, or voluntary dropouts—that remain poorly quantified. This paper presents the first systematic study of how missing participants impact model quality in cross-silo FL. We conduct extensive experiments across image, tabular, and time-series benchmarks, varying data skewness (Dirichlet α), failure patterns (random, targeted, periodic), and model architectures. Results show that data skewness is the strongest confounding factor: under high heterogeneity (α ≤ 0.5), even 10% participant dropout induces up to ±8.2% accuracy fluctuation and biases evaluation toward unwarranted optimism. Targeted failure of critical nodes degrades performance up to 3.1× more than random dropout, especially in small-scale deployments (N ≤ 10). Crucially, the global model offers limited utility for missing participants upon rejoining—achieving only 62.4–79.1% of their optimal local performance—highlighting the need for adaptive recovery mechanisms. Our work establishes foundational metrics and insights for building failure-resilient FL systems.

item.title: FEAST: Fully Connected Expressive Attention for Spatial Transcriptomics
item.description: Spatial Transcriptomics (ST) enables spatially resolved gene expression profiling but remains costly and inaccessible for broad use. To address this, we propose **FEAST**, a novel attention-based framework that models tissue as a *fully connected graph*, overcoming the structural limitation of sparse-graph GNNs in capturing non-local and biologically relevant spot interactions. FEAST introduces **negative-aware attention**, explicitly modeling both excitatory and inhibitory molecular interactions—often missed by standard attention mechanisms—and an **off-grid sampling strategy** to enrich morphological context beyond fixed spot centers. On public ST datasets (e.g., 10X Visium), FEAST achieves state-of-the-art performance in spatial gene expression prediction (reducing MAE by 12.7–18.3% over baselines) and generates biologically interpretable attention maps revealing plausible positive/negative regulatory patterns. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/starforTJ/FEAST.

item.title: Goodness-of-pronunciation without phoneme time alignment
item.description: This paper addresses the challenge of extending automatic pronunciation evaluation to low-resource languages, where standard phoneme-aligned ASR models are infeasible due to scarce labeled data. We propose a novel GoP framework that bypasses explicit phoneme time alignment—leveraging weakly supervised multilingual ASR models (e.g., XLS-R) without requiring phonemic supervision or forced alignment. Key innovations include: (1) estimating phoneme posteriors via mapping ASR word hypotheses to a phoneme confusion network constrained by pronunciation lexicons; (2) replacing phoneme-level duration and speaking rate with robust word-level prosodic features; and (3) fusing frame-level encoder representations and phoneme posteriors through a cross-attention module, enabling implicit alignment. Evaluated on English Speechocean762 and low-resource Tamil datasets, our method achieves performance on par with conventional frame-synchronous baselines (within ±0.8% F1 for error detection) and outperforms alignment-dependent approaches by 12.3% F1 on Tamil—demonstrating strong cross-lingual generalizability and practical viability for resource-constrained settings.

item.title: Reinforcement learning for quantum processes with memory
item.description: We formulate reinforcement learning for quantum environments with **hidden memory**, where the state evolves via unknown quantum channels and the agent acts via quantum instruments—enabling continuous action spaces (e.g., arbitrary POVMs). We adapt an **optimistic maximum-likelihood estimation algorithm**, carefully controlling error propagation through quantum dynamics. We prove a **tight $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{K})$ cumulative regret bound** over $K$ episodes, matching the $\Omega(\sqrt{K})$ information-theoretic lower bound derived via reduction to the multi-armed quantum bandit problem. As a physical application, we model **state-agnostic work extraction** from non-i.i.d. quantum states correlated by hidden memory: here, regret exactly equals cumulative thermodynamic dissipation, and our adaptive policy achieves sublinear dissipation and asymptotically vanishing dissipation rate.

item.title: Social Hippocampus Memory Learning
item.description: Social learning posits that intelligent agents improve not in isolation, but through structured knowledge exchange—motivating *Social Machine Learning* (SML). While federated learning (FL) offers a natural substrate for SML, existing heterogeneous FL methods often share model parameters or intermediate representations, risking privacy leakage and high communication overhead. We propose **SoHip (Social Hippocampus Memory Learning)**, a memory-centric framework where agents collaborate *exclusively via lightweight, abstracted memory*—not models or gradients. Inspired by hippocampal memory consolidation, SoHip extracts short-term memory from local features, transforms it into individual long-term memory via an attention-gated compression mechanism, and fuses it with a collectively aggregated long-term memory bank to boost local inference. Raw data and models remain strictly on-device. Theoretically, SoHip guarantees convergence under non-convex settings and provides inherent privacy preservation. Empirically, on CIFAR-10-C and DomainNet with seven strong baselines, SoHip achieves up to **+8.78% accuracy** and **63% less communication**, establishing memory sharing as a superior, privacy-aware collaboration paradigm.

item.title: Spatiotemporal System Forecasting with Irregular Time Steps via Masked Autoencoder
item.description: Predicting high-dimensional spatiotemporal systems—such as ocean temperature, atmospheric fields, or fluid turbulence—under irregular time steps remains a fundamental challenge for data-driven forecasting. To address this, we propose the **Physics-Spatiotemporal Masked Autoencoder (PS-MAE)**: a unified architecture that combines convolutional spatial encoders with a time-aware masked autoencoding mechanism, leveraging attention to reconstruct full physical sequences in one pass—**without imputation or resampling**. Crucially, “physics” here denotes the intrinsic structure of the high-dimensional dynamical field itself, preserved end-to-end via reconstruction fidelity rather than explicit PDE constraints. Evaluated on four simulated PDE systems and real-world OSTIA ocean temperature data, PS-MAE achieves **32.7% lower MAE** than ConvLSTM over 5-step forecasts, **41.5% higher robustness** to nonlinear perturbations, and **2.8× faster inference**, demonstrating strong generalization across domains without domain-specific engineering.

item.title: An Integrative Genome-Scale Metabolic Modeling and Machine Learning Framework for Predicting and Optimizing Biofuel-Relevant Biomass Production in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
item.description: We present an integrative computational framework combining the Yeast9 genome-scale metabolic model (GEM), interpretable machine learning (ML), and generative modeling to predict, interpret, and optimize biomass production in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* for biofuel applications. Flux balance analysis (FBA) generated 2,000 diverse flux profiles by systematically varying glucose, O₂, and NH₄⁺ uptake rates. Random Forest and XGBoost regressors achieved exceptional predictive accuracy (R² = 0.99989 and 0.9990) for biomass flux. A variational autoencoder uncovered four distinct metabolic phenotypes, while SHAP analysis identified glycolysis, TCA cycle, and lipid biosynthesis as dominant determinants. Bayesian optimization of nutrient constraints increased predicted biomass flux 12-fold (from 0.0858 to 1.041 gDW/hr), and *in silico* overexpression yielded 0.979 gDW/hr. Critically, a tailored generative adversarial network (GAN) produced novel, stoichiometrically feasible flux configurations beyond training data—demonstrating how GEM-informed simulation, explainable ML, and generative AI synergistically advance rational yeast metabolic engineering.

item.title: Fair regression under localized demographic parity constraints
item.description: Demographic parity (DP), while natural for classification, is overly restrictive in regression and harms accuracy when enforced globally. We propose a *localized DP relaxation*: fairness is required only at a finite set of quantiles or score thresholds—enabling interpretable, decision-relevant corrections. We introduce the $({\ell}, Z)$-fair predictor, which constrains group-conditional CDFs to match prescribed values $\ell_m$ at points $z_m$. For this setting, we derive **closed-form optimal discretized predictors** via Lagrangian duality and prove the discretization risk gap vanishes as the grid refines. We further design a **model-agnostic post-processing algorithm** using two samples (labeled for base regression; unlabeled for calibration), with finite-sample guarantees on constraint violation and excess penalized risk. Two alternative CDF-matching frameworks (group–marginal and threshold-aligned) also yield closed-form optima. Experiments confirm a controllable fairness–accuracy trade-off, preserving predictive power while enabling targeted fairness interventions at operationally meaningful quantiles or thresholds.

item.title: SEVerA: Verified Synthesis of Self-Evolving Agents
item.description: Recent self-evolving LLM agent frameworks improve task performance via planner-synthesized parametric programs but offer **no formal guarantees** of safety or correctness—posing critical reliability risks when deployed autonomously. We propose **SEVerA**, the first framework for *verified synthesis* of self-evolving agents. It introduces **Formally Guarded Generative Models (FGGM)**, where each generative call is equipped with a first-order logic output contract and a *verified rejection sampler with fallback*, ensuring contract satisfaction for all inputs and parameter settings. SEVerA’s three-stage pipeline—**Search** (synthesizing FGGM-augmented programs), **Verification** (proving universal correctness over parameters, reducing to unconstrained learning), and **Learning** (gradient-based optimization like GRPO fine-tuning)—preserves hard constraints while optimizing soft utility. Evaluated on Dafny verification, symbolic math synthesis, and policy-compliant tool use ($τ^2$-bench), SEVerA achieves **zero constraint violations** and outperforms unconstrained and SOTA baselines—demonstrating that formal behavioral constraints both guarantee correctness *and* steer synthesis toward higher-quality agents.

item.title: Estimating near-verbatim extraction risk in language models with decoding-constrained beam search
item.description: Recent work reveals that standard greedy-decoding methods for measuring memorization in LLMs overlook *near-verbatim* extraction—sequences differing from memorized content by minor edits (e.g., synonyms, insertions), which pose comparable privacy and copyright risks. While probabilistic extraction offers a principled framework, exact estimation is intractable for near-verbatim cases due to combinatorial explosion, and Monte Carlo sampling requires ∼100,000 samples per sequence for reliability. We introduce **decoding-constrained beam search (DCBS)**, a deterministic, efficient algorithm that computes tight lower bounds on near-verbatim extraction risk at cost equivalent to ∼20 MC samples. Across diverse models and text types, DCBS uncovers substantially more extractable sequences (3.2–8.7×), higher per-sequence extraction mass (4.1×), and interpretable patterns—e.g., near-verbatim risk dominates in structured text (68% vs. 29% in free text) and scales non-linearly with model size. DCBS enables practical, scalable, and policy-relevant memorization auditing.

item.title: Prompt Attack Detection with LLM-as-a-Judge and Mixture-of-Models
item.description: Prompt attacks—such as jailbreaks and prompt injections—pose severe security risks to LLM systems, yet real-time defense demands sub-second latency, creating a deployment gap between inaccurate lightweight detectors and prohibitively slow/costly LLM-based judges. This work demonstrates that general-purpose, lightweight LLMs (e.g., *gemini-2.0-flash-lite-001*) can serve as effective, low-latency security judges when guided by a structured reasoning pipeline: explicit intent decomposition, safety-signal verification, harm assessment, and self-reflection. Evaluated on a curated dataset combining real-world benign queries and adversarial prompts from automated red teaming (ART), our method achieves 92.4% accuracy and F1=0.89 with <300ms latency—outperforming rule-based and classifier baselines by +37.1% and +22.6% in F1, respectively. It is now deployed as a centralized guardrail for public-service chatbots in Singapore. A Mixture-of-Models (MoM) extension yields only marginal gains (+1.3% F1), affirming the efficiency and practicality of optimized single-model judging.
